requirement: General description of the AI system
reasoning: The ontology clearly models the AI system itself, its provider (via the AI Provider class and the is provided by relation) and a generic version attribute, which satisfies two of the three competency questions. However, there is no explicit property for capturing the intended purpose of the system, nor a dedicated construct to express relationships between successive versions (e.g., previous or successor versions), so the requirement is only partially covered.
missing: hasPurpose / intendedPurpose (property linking an AI System to its declared purpose)
hasPreviousVersion / followsVersion / versionLineage (property to relate a system version with its predecessor(s))

reasoning: The ontology includes properties and classes that capture internal software dependencies (depends on, Software Dependency) and required hardware (requires hardware, Hardware Component), as well as a generic Interface linking the AI system to users or other systems. However it lacks explicit constructs for external or third‑party AI systems and for modeling interaction relationships beyond simple dependency, so the coverage is good but not complete.
missing: interactsWith (object property linking an AI system to ExternalSystem or other AI systems)
APIEndpoint or ServiceContract (property describing the technical interface for external interaction)
DependencyType (enumeration distinguishing required, optional, runtime, compile‑time dependencies)
ThirdPartySoftwareComponent (class for external libraries/services not part of the AI system)

reasoning: The ontology includes a Software Dependency class to capture the packages/libraries an AI system relies on, and a generic version attribute that can be attached to those dependencies or to software components. This allows identification of which software is used and what its current version is. However, there is no explicit concept for firmware, nor any property describing update policies, required version ranges, or how updates are managed, so the coverage is partial.
missing: Software Firmware (class or subclass of Software Component)
Version Update Policy (property linking a software dependency/component to its update requirements or schedule)
has version constraint (property for specifying permissible version ranges)
is updated by / has update event (activity class capturing the act of updating software/firmware)

reasoning: The ontology contains elements that can describe parts of a provision form (e.g., Interface for APIs, Software Component/Implementation for downloadable or embedded packages, Hardware Component for embedded solutions) and links to the provider. However, it lacks a dedicated concept or property that explicitly captures the distribution modality itself ("embedded", "downloadable", "API as‑a‑service", etc.), which is crucial for answering how the AI system is provided to users.
missing: DistributionForm (or ProvisionMode) – a class representing the way an AI system is made available, with instances such as 'EmbeddedSoftware', 'DownloadablePackage', 'WebAPI', 'SaaS', etc.
has distribution form – object property linking AI System to DistributionForm
DeliveryChannel – optional class for market‑related channels (app store, website, OEM contract, etc.)

reasoning: The ontology provides a dedicated class for hardware (Hardware Component) and explicit relations (requires hardware) that link an AI system to its needed hardware. Additionally, Computational Resource together with properties describing resource type, configuration, and vendor allow detailed descriptions of the intended execution platform. However, there is no single concept that aggregates these details into a formal "hardware specification" or captures performance constraints, so coverage is high but not complete.
missing: "Hardware Specification" class to encapsulate full hardware requirements (e.g., CPU/GPU models, memory, storage, accelerators).
"has performance requirement" property for minimum capabilities such as GPU memory or compute throughput.
"deployment platform" concept to distinguish on‑premise, cloud, edge, etc., and link it to the hardware description.

reasoning: The existing ontology contains generic notions of physical hardware (Hardware Component) and tangible artefacts (AI Artifact) that could be stretched to represent photos or diagrams, and it can express that a system "has component" or "has architecture" for internal arrangement. However, there are no dedicated concepts for the product's physical form, external markings (e.g., CE label), visual documentation of external features, nor for structured descriptions of interior layout. Consequently only a small fraction of the requirement is covered.

requirement: User interface and instructions for use
reasoning: The ontology includes a class (Interface) and a property (has interface) that can capture the description of the user‑interface provided to the deployer, as well as agents such as AI Deployer linked via is deployed by. However, there is no concept or property for the “instructions for use” (e.g., manuals, guides, safety notices), so the competency question about what instructions are provided cannot be answered directly.
missing: InstructionDocument (or UsageGuide) – a class representing deployment/operation instructions
has instruction / provides documentation – a property linking an AI System or Interface to an InstructionDocument

requirement: Elements of system and development process
reasoning: The ontology provides classes for the activities (AI Activity) that describe development steps and for the artefacts used or produced (AI Model, Dataset, Software Dependency, Software Implementation, Software Component). Provenance‑style relations such as 'depends on' and 'feeds into component' capture integration of components, while 'has component' and 'has architecture' record the composition of the system. However, there is no explicit notion of a "pre‑trained model" or "third‑party tool", nor concepts describing how those artefacts are modified or customized during development, which limits full compliance with the requirement.
missing: ThirdPartyTool (subclass of Software Component or Software Dependency)
used by / modified by relationships linking provider to third‑party artefacts

reasoning: The existing ontology can capture structural aspects of the system (e.g., AI System, its architecture, components, datasets and outputs) and some provenance information (provider, deployer). However it lacks explicit constructs for design choices, rationale, assumptions, classification decisions, optimisation objectives, parameter relevance, output‑quality metrics, trade‑off documentation, and the intended user groups required by the AI Act requirement. Consequently only about half of the competency questions can be answered directly.
missing: DesignChoice (class) – with properties: hasRationale, hasAssumption, relatesToComponent
ClassificationChoice (class) – to capture algorithmic or label‑scheme decisions
OutputQualityMetric (class) – describing expected quality dimensions (accuracy, latency, robustness)
TradeOffDecision (class) – linking technicalSolution to complianceRequirement and expressing trade‑off rationale
IntendedUserGroup (class) – representing persons or groups the system is meant for
ComplianceReference (class/property) – to point to specific legal clauses such as Chapter III Section 2

reasoning: The ontology contains classes for software components, their inter‑connections (feeds into component), architectural descriptions, AI models/algorithms and computational resources with detailed attributes, covering the main competency questions. However, it lacks explicit constructs for pipeline stages or activities linked to specific resource usage (e.g., training, testing, validation) and a dedicated 'Algorithm' concept, so coverage is high but not complete.
missing: ProcessingStep (or PipelineStage) class to model individual steps in the processing pipeline
Algorithm (or Method) class distinct from AI Model for describing used algorithms
ActivityResourceUsage property linking Computational Resource to specific AI Activity instances such as training, testing and validation

reasoning: The ontology includes generic classes for datasets and activities, which can be linked to the training process via 'AI Activity' and 'has component'. However, it lacks explicit properties or subclasses that capture provenance details, selection criteria, cleaning methods, labeling procedures, and datasheet documentation required by the AI Act. Consequently, only a partial alignment is achieved.

The ontology contains entities that identify who deploys the system (AI Deployer) and the user‑facing artefact (Interface) with a property linking them to the AI system. However, it lacks explicit concepts for human‑oversight measures and technical means to make outputs interpretable, which are central to the requirement. Consequently, only partial coverage is achieved.
missing: has oversight measure (property linking AI System to HumanOversightMeasure)
supports interpretability (property linking Interface or Software Component to TechnicalInterpretabilityMeasure)

requirement: Pre-determined changes to the AI system
reasoning: The ontology contains elements to describe the AI system, its components and versions, and activities performed by providers or deployers, which can be used to model a change event. However, it lacks explicit constructs for pre‑determined changes, performance impact expectations, and the technical solutions (e.g., monitoring, audit) required for continuous compliance, so coverage is only partial.

The ontology provides generic classes for activities, datasets and artifacts that can be reused to model validation/testing steps, the data used and test documentation, as well as agents who could sign reports. However it lacks explicit concepts for validation procedures, test logs/reports, metrics (accuracy, robustness, compliance, discrimination), dataset characteristics, dates/signatures, and change‑tracking, so the requirement is only partially covered.
missing: Metric (with sub‑classes AccuracyMetric, RobustnessMetric, ComplianceMetric, DiscriminationImpactMetric)
ResponsiblePerson (subclass of AI Agent) with properties dateSigned and signedBy

reasoning: The provided ontology contains classes for system components (e.g., Software Component, Hardware Component) and relationships describing dependencies, but it has no explicit class or property to capture cybersecurity measures or security controls required by the AI Act. Consequently, the requirement cannot be directly answered using the current terms.
missing: has security measure (object property linking AI System or Component to CybersecurityMeasure)
mitigates risk (property relating SecurityControl to identified risks)

reasoning: The ontology contains core constructs for the AI system, its models, data and deployment context, which can be linked to monitoring or oversight information. However, it lacks explicit concepts for performance capabilities, accuracy per demographic group, risk and unintended‑outcome assessments, human‑oversight procedures, and technical interpretability measures required by the AI Act, resulting in limited coverage.

The ontology contains generic classes for the AI system itself (AI System) and for lifecycle actions (AI Activity), which could be used to model an evaluation step, but it lacks any explicit class or property for performance metrics, their selection, or a description of why those metrics are appropriate. Consequently only very high‑level elements can be linked, giving minimal coverage.
missing: PerformanceMetric (class representing a concrete metric such as accuracy, F1‑score, etc.)
EvaluationActivity (subclass of AI Activity for model testing/validation)
hasPerformanceMetric (property linking AI System or EvaluationActivity to PerformanceMetric)
metricAppropriatenessDescription (property providing the narrative justification for metric choice)
AssessmentReport (class for documents that capture evaluation results and appropriateness statements)

reasoning: The present ontology lacks any concept that directly represents a risk‑management system, its procedures, or compliance documentation required by Article 9. Only very generic elements such as AI Activity (which could be used to model the risk‑management process) and structural links like has component/has architecture can be stretched to capture parts of a description, resulting in low coverage.

requirement: Changes to the AI system throughout its lifecycle
reasoning: The ontology provides AI Activity (a prov:Activity subclass) to model lifecycle steps and a version property to record different system releases, which together can capture many types of changes made by the provider. However, there is no dedicated concept for a "change" or explicit properties for change description, timestamp, or linkage between an activity and the specific artifact it modified, so the requirement is only partially covered.
missing: Change (or Modification) class: to represent a concrete alteration made to the AI system
hasChangeDescription property: free‑text description of what was changed
changeTimestamp property: when the change occurred
wasPerformedBy / wasAppliedTo provenance properties linking Change, AI Activity, and AI System

reasoning: The existing ontology only provides very generic provenance entities (AI Artifact) that could be used to model documents describing standards or compliance solutions, and a generic 'version' property that might record the version of a standard. No specific classes or properties capture the notion of harmonised standards, their application status, references to Chapter III‑Section 2, or alternative technical specifications, so coverage of the requirement is minimal.

The ontology includes the generic class AI Artifact, which could be used to model a copy of the EU declaration of conformity as an entity. However, there is no specific class for a compliance or declaration document, nor any property that relates such a document to an AI system, provider, or deployer. Consequently the coverage is very limited.
missing: hasDeclaration (object property linking AI System / AI Provider to DeclarationOfConformity)
ComplianceDocument (alternative generic class for regulatory artefacts)
is attached to / documents (property to express that a document is associated with an AI system)

reasoning: The existing ontology can represent the AI system itself (AI System) and can model activities (AI Activity) that could be used to describe monitoring actions, but it lacks dedicated concepts for a post‑market monitoring plan or a performance‑evaluation mechanism. Consequently only very generic terms match, leaving most of the requirement uncovered.
